title: Apply: First Amendment: Religion
description: Apply your knowledge for the First Amendment's religious clauses to this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from George Washington’s 1790 letter to the Hebrew Congregation at Newport, Rhode Island.**

It is now no more than toleration is spoken of, as if it was by the [[☃ definition 1]] of one class of people, that another enjoyed the exercise of their inherent natural rights. For happily the Government of the United States, which gives to bigotry no [[☃ definition 2]], to [[☃ definition 3]] no assistance, only that those who live under its protection should [conduct] themselves as good citizens, in giving it on all occasions their effectual support.

**Which statement best summarizes Washington’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: treating someone unfairly or cruelly, especially because of their religion or identity

choices.content: Washington suggests the government does have a role in preventing bigotry or persecution.
The government must protect people’s natural rights, and if it doesn’t, the people can replace the government.
The government will protect people’s religious beliefs, so long as the people support his political party.
The government does not allow religious prejudice, regardless of people’s particular religious beliefs.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from George Washington’s 1790 letter to the Hebrew Congregation at Newport, Rhode Island.**

It is now no more than toleration is spoken of, as if it was by the [[☃ definition 1]] of one class of people, that another enjoyed the exercise of their inherent natural rights. For happily the Government of the United States, which gives to bigotry no [[☃ definition 2]], to [[☃ definition 3]] no assistance, only that those who live under its protection should [conduct] themselves as good citizens, in giving it on all occasions their effectual support.

**The ideas in the excerpt are best reflected in which protection listed in the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: treating someone unfairly or cruelly, especially because of their religion or identity

choices.content: The right to petition the government
The right to have any beliefs
The right to speak freely
The right to assemble in public

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Virginia Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That religion, or the duty which we owe to our Creator and the manner of [[☃ definition 1]] it, can be directed by reason and [[☃ definition 2]], not by force or violence; and therefore, all men are equally entitled to the free exercise of religion, according to the [[☃ definition 3]] of conscience; and that it is the mutual duty of all to practice Christian [[☃ definition 4]], love, and charity towards each other.

**The ideas in the excerpt were meant to address which problem that the colonists faced under British rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: patience and tolerance in response to something that you disagree with

choices.content: The inclusion of religious beliefs in colonial charters
The taxation  of the colonies without representation
The mistreatment of individuals and groups based on their faith
The government’s censorship of certain newspapers

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Virginia Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That religion, or the duty which we owe to our Creator and the manner of [[☃ definition 1]] it, can be directed by reason and [[☃ definition 2]], not by force or violence...

**The language in this excerpt most closely resembles the language of which part of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Establishment Clause
The Press Clause
The Assembly Clause
The Petition Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Abington School District v. Schempp* (1963) written by Justice Tom C. Clark.**

Applying the Establishment Clause principles to the cases [[☃ definition 1]], we find that the States are requiring the selection and reading at the opening of the school day of verses from the Holy Bible and the recitation of the Lord's Prayer by the students in unison. These exercises are [[☃ definition 2]] as part of the curricular activities of students who are required by law to attend school. They are held in the school buildings under the supervision and with the participation of teachers employed in those schools. . . .We agree with the trial court's finding as to the religious character of the exercises. Given that finding, the exercises and the law requiring them are in violation of the Establishment Clause.

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s opinion in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The government cannot limit attendance at religious services, even during a public health crisis.
State-run institutions, like government buildings, cannot display religious symbols in public spaces.
The government must provide spaces in government buildings for people to pray privately.
State-run institutions, like schools, cannot force students to participate in organized religious practices.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Abington School District v. Schempp* (1963) written by Justice Tom C. Clark.**

Applying the Establishment Clause principles to the cases [[☃ definition 1]], we find that the States are requiring the selection and reading at the opening of the school day of verses from the Holy Bible and the recitation of the Lord's Prayer by the students in unison. These exercises are [[☃ definition 2]] as part of the curricular activities of students who are required by law to attend school. They are held in the school buildings under the supervision and with the participation of teachers employed in those schools. . . .We agree with the trial court's finding as to the religious character of the exercises. Given that finding, the exercises and the law requiring them are in violation of the Establishment Clause.

**The reasoning in this excerpt most closely mirrors the Court’s opinion in which case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Reynolds v. United States* (1879)
*Engel v. Vitale* (1962)
*Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)
*Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn v. Cuomo* (2020)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Frankfurter’s opinion in *Minersville School District Board of Education v. Gobitis* (1940).**

[[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] have not, in the course of the long struggle for religious toleration, relieved the individual from obedience to a general law not aimed at the promotion or restriction of religious beliefs. The mere possession of religious [[☃ definition 3]] which contradict the relevant concerns of a political society does not relieve the citizen from the discharge of political responsibilities.

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s opinion in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: influenced by one's beliefs about right and wrong
moral concerns that keep you from doing something

choices.content: People must follow laws if the laws do not encourage or discourage a specific religion.
The state cannot force people to perform actions that violate their religious beliefs.
People can pray in state-run institutions, so long as the prayer is an individual action.
The government cannot display religious symbols on state-owned property.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Frankfurter’s opinion in *Minersville School District Board of Education v. Gobitis* (1940).**

[[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] have not, in the course of the long struggle for religious toleration, relieved the individual from obedience to a general law not aimed at the promotion or restriction of religious beliefs. The mere possession of religious [[☃ definition 3]] which contradict the relevant concerns of a political society does not relieve the citizen from the discharge of political responsibilities.

**The reasoning in this excerpt most closely mirrors the Court’s opinion in which case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: influenced by one's beliefs about right and wrong
moral concerns that keep you from doing something

choices.content: *Engel v. Vitale* (1962)
*Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)
*Employment Division v. Smith* (1990)
*Kennedy v. Bremerton School District* (2022)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Maryland Declaration of Rights of 1776.**

That, as it is the duty of every man to worship God in such manner as he thinks most acceptable to him; all persons, [[☃ definition 1]] the Christian religion, are equally entitled to protection in their religious liberty; [[☃ definition 2]] no person ought by any law to be [[☃ definition 3]] in his person or estate on account of his religious persuasion or profession, or for his religious practice; unless, [[☃ definition 4]] using religion, any man shall disturb the good order, peace or safety of the State, or shall [[☃ definition 5]] the laws of morality, or injure others, in their natural, civil, or religious rights. . . .

**This excerpt serves as evidence of which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: claiming to follow or believe in

choices.content: Some American colonies protected the free exercise of religion before the ratification of the First Amendment.
Some American colonies established a religion that all citizens were required to follow.
Some American colonies required citizens to pay a tax to a religious institution to cover operational costs.
Some American colonies prohibited members of certain Christian churches from engaging in their religious practices.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter by Oliver Ellsworth and published in the *Connecticut Courant* in 1787. **
 
Some very worthy persons, who have not had great advantages for information, have objected against that clause in the constitution which provides that no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. They have been afraid that this clause is unfavorable to religion. 

But my countrymen, the sole purpose and effect of it is to exclude [[☃ definition 1]], and to secure to you the important right of religious liberty. We are almost the only people in the world who have a full enjoyment of this important right of human nature. In our country every man has a right to worship God in that way which is most agreeable to his conscience. If he be a good and peaceable person, he is [[☃ definition 2]] to no penalties or [[☃ definition 3]] on account of his religious sentiments; or in other words, he is not subject to persecution.

**Which statement best summarizes Ellsworth’s argument about the No Religious Test Clause in Article VI of the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It unfairly punishes religious people, as they have to promise not to use their religious beliefs to make governmental decisions.
It protects religious liberty and prevents discrimination, allowing government officials to worship freely without fear of penalties.
It leads to immorality and unethical behavior in government, as there are no moral standards for people who serve in public office.
It ensures that all religious groups are equally represented in government, guaranteeing proportional influence based on size.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Edmund Randolph’s 1788 speech at the Virginia Ratifying Convention.**

Freedom of religion is said to be in danger. I will [[☃ definition 1]] say, I once thought that it was, and felt great [[☃ definition 2]] to the constitution for that reason. I am willing to acknowledge my [[☃ definition 3]] removed. . . . 

I am a friend to a variety of [[☃ definition 4]], because they keep one another in order. How many different sects are we composed of throughout the United States? How many different sects will be in congress? We cannot [[☃ definition 5]] the sects that may be in congress. And there are so many now in the United States that they will prevent the establishment of any one sect in prejudice to the rest, and will forever oppose all attempts to [[☃ definition 6]] religious liberty. If such an attempt be made, will not the alarm be sounded throughout America? If congress be as wicked as we are foretold they will, they would not run the risk of exciting the resentment of all, or most of the religious sects in America.

**Randolph would most likely agree with which statement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: religious groups that have specific sets of beliefs

choices.content: The state constitutions already protect minority religions from persecution, and no state has a law stating that all people must follow a specific religion.
The federal government lacks the power to determine the national religion, which will lead to different sects fighting with each other in Congress.
Because there is already great religious diversity in the U.S., the Constitution does not need to be amended to prevent Congress from establishing a national religion.
Congress’s increased power under the Constitution is dangerous, as Congress could require every person to follow the same religion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Rights of Conscience Inalienable,” a 1791 sermon by John Leland.**

Is [[☃ definition 1]] of [[☃ definition 2]] in matters of religion essential to the happiness of civil government? Not at all. Government has no more to do with the religious opinions of men than it has with the principles of the mathematics. Let every man speak freely without fear—maintain the principles that he believes—worship according to his own faith, either one God, three Gods, no God, or twenty Gods; and let government protect him in so doing, [[☃ definition 3]], see that he meets with no personal abuse or loss of property for his religious opinions.

**Which statement best summarizes Leland’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: uniformity; when everyone thinks or acts in the same way
abbreviation for the Latin phrase *id est*, meaning “that is”; used to restate or clarify the previous statement

choices.content: It is the government’s job to financially support the creation of places of worship across the United States.
It is the government’s job to choose a religion that all people in the United States should follow.
It is the government’s job to pass laws that align with the principles of the most popular religions.
It is the government’s job to protect people’s right to choose whether to follow any particular religious doctrine.
